data.title: APA promotes Ben Rodgers to the post of CFO
data.description:
APA Corporation (NASDAQ:APA) announced on Monday the promotion of Ben Rodgers to the post of executive vice president and CFO, effective May 12.

Rodgers previously served as senior vice president, finance and treasurer.

data.title: MarketAxess reports record trading week in U.S. government bonds
data.description:
• MarketAxess Holdings (NASDAQ:MKTX) has announced that its Rates business reported an all-time single-day record of $102.3B U.S. Government Bonds notional traded on April 9.
  • “We’re thrilled to see this growth in our client business. We’re seeing clients increasingly seek out firm sources of liquidity like Live Markets to weather periods of market volatility like we experienced last week,” said Chris Good, Head of Rates at MarketAxess.
  • Following the launch of an expanded algo suite for Rates, MarketAxess also plans to introduce enhanced Request for Quote for U.S. Government Bonds, allowing dealers expanded reach through the MarketAxess client network.

data.title: Dollar edges higher after last week’s slump-Currency Recap
data.description:
The U.S. Dollar edged higher on Monday, walking back on some of its losses from last week, as markets scrambled to assess the impact of Trump’s uncertain tariff moves on the world’s reserve currency. 

The greenback was last up 0.14%, at $100.03, as Trump announced late on Friday that smartphones, computers, and other electronics would be temporarily exempted from tariffs. 

However, Trump said that the exemption was only temporary and that he would unveil tariffs on imported semiconductors soon. 

Dollar weekly moves and key drivers: 

The U.S. Dollar Index (DXY), which measures the value of the dollar against a basket of six foreign currencies, fell 3.26% over the last week. 

The greenback saw a sharp decline last week as investors fled U.S. assets due to tariff uncertainties. Although Trump announced a 90-day pause on tariffs for most countries, China still faces a significantly higher tariff rate, contributing to market volatility. 

China retaliated last week with a plan to raise tariffs on U.S. imports to 125%, from the previous 84%. This sent the U.S. dollar below $100 on Friday, reaching its lowest level since mid-2023. 

Adding pressure to the U.S. dollar, US Labor Department data showed an unexpected decline in consumer prices for March, and U.S. Treasury yields also felt the burn.

The benchmark U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) hit a two-month high on Friday, amid a bruising bond selloff in longer-term maturities. The US10Y surged as much as 19 basis points to hit a session peak at 4.58%, its highest level since February 12. 

Meanwhile, the shorter-end, 2-year yield (US2Y) also rose 19 points over the week to 3.94%. 

Currency Highlights: Major currency movements (April 7 to April 13) 

Euro (EUR:USD)

+3.72%

Pound Sterling (GBP:USD)

+2.58%

Japanese Yen (JPY:USD)

+3.10%

Chinese Yuan (CNY:USD)

+0.40%

Swiss Franc (CHF:USD)

+4.93%

Australian Dollar (AUD:USD)

+4.31%

Canadian Dollar (CAD:USD)

+2.28%

The Euro (EUR:USD) was last down 0.02% against the U.S. Dollar, but hovered close to Friday’s three-year high. The euro gained more than 3% during the last week as investors looked for a safe haven as confidence eroded in the dollar. 

Euro traders will keep a close eye on the European Central Bank policymakers’ latest meeting this week on interest rate decision. 

The British Pound (GBP:USD) was last up 0.33% against the greenback, extending its gains from last week, on the back of a weaker dollar. The pound will move further this week as the latest UK economic data comes in, such as unemployment and inflation numbers. 

The Japanese yen (JPY:USD) was up 0.39% against the dollar on Monday, as investors kept looking at the currency as a potential safe haven. 

Market reactions and analysis:

Deutsche Bank said last week that it sees the end of an era for the U.S. dollar. “We are witnessing a simultaneous collapse in the price of all U.S. assets including equities, the dollar versus alternative reserve FX and the bond market,” said George Saravelos, co-head of Foreign Exchange Research at Deutsche Bank. 

“In a typical crisis environment, the market would be hoarding dollar liquidity to secure funding for its underlying US asset base,” he said. However, the market seems to “have lost faith” in U.S. assets, and instead of buying them, they are being sold, he added.

Meanwhile, according to BofA, investors should short the S&P 500 until the Federal Reserve “cuts hard” to break the liquidation cycle, and until the U.S. and China pause their trade war and reverse global recession momentum.

BofA sees U.S. yields going higher and recommends investors to long the 2-year U.S. Treasuries (US2Y) and to short the S&P 500 (SP500) until the 4,800 level. 

Economic Indicators to watch: 

Investors will look out for retail sales data for March, which is due to come out this week. Other important economic data due this week, include housing market data, housing starts, and homebuilder confidence survey. 

Markets will also await earnings from several large financial firms this week, such as Goldman Sachs, Bank of America, and Citigroup, including results from Netflix, TSMC, United Airlines, and UnitedHealth Group. 

Dollar-Based ETFs: (UUP), (USDU), and (UDN).

data.title: Biggest stock movers Monday: Tech stocks, DVA, and more
data.description:
Stock futures rose on Monday after Trump’s new “reciprocal” tariffs exempted tech products like smartphones and chips, though officials warned the exemptions may not last.

Here are some of Monday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) +5.5% – Tech stocks saw a surge after the Trump administration temporarily excluded smartphones, laptops, and key electronics components from its new 145% reciprocal tariffs on Chinese goods. The U.S. Customs and Border Protection issued the exemptions late Friday, providing relief to tech stocks, though both President Trump and Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick warned that these exemptions may not be permanent. Other notable gainers included Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) +3%, Dell Technologies (NYSE:DELL) +7%, Super Micro Computer (NASDAQ:SMCI) +5.7%, Hewlett Packard Enterprise (NYSE:HPE) +5%, and HP (NYSE:HPQ) +6%.
  • BP (NYSE:BP) +4% - Shares rose after the company announced the discovery of oil at the U.S. Far South prospect in the Gulf of Mexico. The discovery, which was made approximately 120 miles off the Louisiana coast at a depth of 23,830 feet, is part of BP’s strategy to increase its upstream production. Initial data suggests the presence of high-quality reservoirs and potentially commercial volumes of hydrocarbons. BP's revised strategy aims to increase its global upstream production to between 2.3M and 2.5M barrels of oil equivalent per day by 2030, with plans for further growth through 2035. The company also intends to drill around 40 wells over the next three years, with 10 to 15 wells expected to be drilled in 2025, underscoring its renewed focus on exploration.
  • Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) +2.5% – Shares rose after the company announced it would not sell Polish broadcaster TVN, putting an end to speculation surrounding the bidding process. In a statement on Monday, WBD explained that, following a strategic review of options for TVN, it had concluded that retaining ownership was the best path forward. The company emphasized its commitment to supporting its business and strategy, along with the journalistic efforts of its team. The decision comes amid financial market volatility triggered by U.S. President Donald Trump’s tariff policies, which have caused many investors to pause deal-making activities.
  • DuPont de Nemours (DD) +3% – Shares climbed after KeyBanc upgraded the stock to Overweight from Sector Weight, citing the company’s strong balance sheet as a key factor in its ability to navigate a potential economic downturn. Analyst Aleksey Yefremov highlighted DuPont’s electronics and water segments as "world-class secular growth franchises" that are currently trading at unusually discounted valuations.
  • Palantir (PLTR) +6% – Shares rose after NATO’s Communications and Information Agency finalized the acquisition to deploy Palantir’s Maven Smart System across Allied Command Operations. The AI-driven system aims to enhance battlefield decision-making and will be operational within 30 days. NATO emphasized the rapid six-month timeline from concept to implementation. NCIA General Manager Ludwig Decamps called it a “state-of-the-art AI capability” that equips forces to operate effectively in modern conflict zones.
  • Viking Therapeutics (VKTX) +9% – U.S. obesity drugmakers rallied after Pfizer said it would halt development of its obesity pill, danuglipron, over a potential drug-related liver injury. While the side effect resolved after stopping treatment, the company decided to exit the program following a data review and regulatory feedback. Structure Therapeutics (GPCR) +7%, Terns Pharmaceuticals (TERN) +5%, Altimmune (ALT) +3%, and Novo Nordisk (NVO) +2%.

Biggest stock losers

  • DaVita (DVA) -5% – Shares fell after the dialysis provider disclosed a ransomware attack affecting some operations. The company said it has isolated impacted systems, engaged third-party cybersecurity experts, and notified law enforcement, but it cannot yet estimate the scope or duration of the disruption.

data.title: UoM survey: Consumer confidence is as weak as it was during the GFC
data.description:
Consumer confidence is now weaker than it was during the 2008 financial crisis, a new UoM survey showed.

The University of Michigan’s consumer sentiment survey showed that it is declining rapidly for households making more than $100,000 as well as for those making less than $100,000.

Confidence for households making less than $100,000 reached about 45%, and for those making more than $100,000, it was a little more than 50%.

 

 

 

Also, household income expectations are declining, while inflation expectations are rising at an unprecedented speed. The expected change in household income in one year is about 0.25%, and the 12-month inflation expectation median increase was recorded by respondents at 6.75%.

Worries about losing their jobs are plaguing consumers at levels normally seen during recessions, reaching almost 70%.

Also, the number of consumers thinking business conditions are worsening has reached a record high. About 65% of consumers believe conditions will worsen in one year.

“The bottom line is that consumer sentiment is very weak, and the fear is that this will spill over to weaker actual spending,” said Torsten Slok, chief economist at Apollo, in his report.

data.title: Tech stocks rebound on Trump tariff exemptions
data.description:
Update:  Adds latest movements.

Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) and other American tech stocks surged on Monday after the Trump administration temporarily exempted smartphones, computers and other electronics, mostly imported from China, from reciprocal tariffs.

The exclusions, published Friday by the U.S. Customs and Border Protection agency, follow Trump's moves to impose 145% tariffs on products from China and 10% baseline tariffs on almost every other nation.

The exemptions apply to popular electronics items such as smartphones, laptops, computers, computer processors, memory chips, flat panel TV displays, solar cells, hard drives.

Apple's (NASDAQ:AAPL) shares rose over 4.5% shortly after market open, while other U.S. tech stocks were also higher with Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) up nearly 1.2%, Dell Technologies (NYSE:DELL) +5.9%, Super Micro Computer (NASDAQ:SMCI) +4.4%, Hewlett Packard Enterprise (NYSE:HPE) +2.7%, HP (NYSE:HPQ) +4.5% and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (NYSE:TSM) -0.59%.

European semiconductor names, such as ASML Holding (NASDAQ:ASML) +1.4%, Infineon (OTCQX:IFNNY) +1% and NXP Semiconductors (NASDAQ:NXPI) +1.7% gained, while STMicroelectronics (NYSE:STM) fell -0.15%.

KeyBanc analyst Brandon Nispel said that the tariff exemption is “probably the best case scenario” for Apple as it “takes a big risk off the table”, raising the recommendation on the stock to Sector Weight from Underweight.

However, Nispel added the stock is not fully “out of the woods” as “growth expectations remain high looking to FY26 and there is likely still consumer spending pullback occurring."

Seeking Alpha's Quant system rates Apple as Hold.

data.title: Watsco rated Equal-weight in new research coverage at Morgan Stanley
data.description:
Watsco (NYSE:WSO) on Monday was rated Equal-weight in new research coverage by analysts at financial-services firm Morgan Stanley. They said the company, the biggest U.S. distributor of residential HVAC equipment and parts, is “best-in-class” but faces softening demand.

“Watsco (NYSE:WSO) has achieved distributor nirvana,” Christopher Snyder, research analyst at Morgan Stanley, said in an April 14 note, pointing to the company’s superior scale and investments in technology that help its customers improve efficiency and profitability.

These tech-driven, value-added services give Watsco (WSO) a competitive edge against smaller, regional rivals, bolstering customer relationships and supporting margin expansion — especially valuable as the U.S. navigates potential new Trump-era tariffs.

Tariff-proof, but not demand-proof

Watsco (WSO) is well-positioned to sustain profitability in an inflationary environment driven by tariffs, Snyder said. The company’s ability to pass on price increases and its strategic inventory holdings give it pricing power and exposure to inventory-related margin gains.

“Every approximate 100 basis points of price drives roughly 2% EPS accretion in an unchanged gross margin scenario,” the bank estimated.

However, the analysts cautioned that near-term momentum could falter due to softening demand in the residential and light commercial HVAC sectors.

“We are cautious on residential and light commercial HVAC end markets over the next twelve months,” Snyder said. “Consumer elasticity remains an under-appreciated risk.”

Since 2019, HVAC prices have surged roughly 40%, with another potential 10% to 20% increase likely in 2025, depending on how tariffs evolve.

The analyst flagged early signs of consumer hesitation to purchase big-ticket items like air conditioners in the face of higher prices and economic uncertainty. Additionally, the upcoming refrigerant regulation changes in 2025 may have pulled forward demand into the second half of 2024 — a trend that Snyder said is more material than industry guidance suggests.

‘Great house’ in slipping neighborhood

Snyder described Watsco (WSO) as a “great house but [the] neighborhood is deteriorating.” Despite the company's attractive fundamentals, including a strong technology platform and dominant market position, the analyst sees limited upside with the stock trading at about 35 times earnings and a 70% premium to the Standard & Poor’s 500 (SP500) stock index.

“While Watsco (WSO) is positioned to extract more value out of the ecosystem … we struggle to find upside,” he said. Gross-margin stability, after years of gradual decline, is already baked into estimates.

Morgan Stanley's $515 price target is based on 31 times 2026–2027 earnings per share, a 55% premium to peers and above the five-year average of 45%, reflecting Watsco’s (WSO) relative strength in a soft growth, inflationary environment.

What could shift rating?

Snyder said he would turn more bullish on Watsco if visibility improved on three key fronts:

  1. Residential HVAC demand rebound,
  2. Channel inventory normalization, and
  3. Gross margin expansion above the current approximate 27% level (with each 100 basis points of gross margin adding about $2 to EPS).

On the flip side, he warned that Watsco’s (WSO) premium positioning could be undermined if competitors begin to replicate its customer-focused tech solutions.

“To take a negative view, we would need to see signs that Watsco’s (WSO) customer proposition is deteriorating,” Snyder said.

While Morgan Stanley holds a neutral stance on Watsco (WSO), Snyder said the company remains among the best-positioned players in a challenging HVAC landscape.

data.title: GCM Grosvenor set to more than double investment in real estate investment firm
data.description:
Urban Standard Capital has expanded its strategic partnership with the alternative asset manager GCM Grosvenor (NASDAQ:GCMG), with the latter set to more than double its investment in the institutional real estate investment firm.

The partnership, structured as a $135M joint venture, will enable Urban Standard to originate more than $1B in loans.

GCM Grosvenor has been a strategic partner of the institutional real estate investment firm for more than two years, and had committed $50M in 2023.

"Middle-market real estate credit and structured equity can provide investors with a compelling opportunity to earn equity-like returns with a credit-level risk profile," noted Urban Standard Founder Seth Weissman.

GCMG shares were trading 1.19% higher Monday pre-market at $12.79.
